content.text: Async code is notoriously difficult to test and debug because the normal mental model of "line A runs, then line B runs" breaks down the moment coroutines start suspending and resuming. The mistakes below are not obvious from reading code alone — they only reveal themselves at runtime, and often only under specific timing conditions.
The single most common mistake in asyncio is calling a coroutine function without
. The coroutine object is created but never scheduled, so the work simply never happens.

async def fetch_data():
    return 42

async def main():
    result = fetch_data()   # BUG: coroutine object, not the return value
    print(result)           # prints: <coroutine object fetch_data at 0x...>
    # Python will warn: RuntimeWarning: coroutine 'fetch_data' was never awaited
. What makes this insidious in tests is that the missing
does not raise an exception — it silently returns a coroutine object, and if your test assertion only checks for a truthy value, it may pass when it should fail (a coroutine object is always truthy).
for unawaited coroutines, but warnings are suppressed by default in many test runners. Enable them explicitly:
but not properly driven by an event loop will pass without ever executing:

import unittest

class MyTests(unittest.TestCase):
    async def test_something(self):   # BUG: never actually runs
        result = await some_coroutine()
        self.assertEqual(result, 42)
sees a coroutine function, calls it to get a coroutine object, and considers the test "passed" because no exception was raised — the coroutine body was never entered. The fix is

# Option 1: pytest-asyncio
import pytest

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_something():
    result = await some_coroutine()
    assert result == 42

# Option 2: IsolatedAsyncioTestCase (stdlib, Python 3.8+)
import unittest

class MyTests(unittest.IsolatedAsyncioTestCase):
    async def test_something(self):
        result = await some_coroutine()
        self.assertEqual(result, 42)
Blocking the Event Loop Inside Tests
Calling any blocking I/O inside an async test stalls the entire event loop for the duration of the call. Network calls, file reads with the standard
, or CPU-heavy computations all fall into this category.

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_data_fetch():
    import time
    time.sleep(2)           # BUG: blocks the event loop for 2 seconds
    result = await fetch()
    assert result is not None
The problem is not just slowness. If other coroutines or tasks are scheduled, they cannot run during the
window. In a test suite that exercises concurrent behavior, this can mask real concurrency bugs. Use

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_data_fetch():
    await asyncio.sleep(0)  # yields control, then continues
    result = await fetch()
    assert result is not None
Mocking Async Functions with Synchronous Mocks
returns a regular object, not a coroutine. If the code under test awaits a mock that is not async, it raises
TypeError: object MagicMock can't be used in 'await' expression

from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_bad_mock():
    with patch('mymodule.fetch', MagicMock(return_value=42)):  # BUG
        result = await mymodule.fetch()  # TypeError at runtime

from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_good_mock():
    with patch('mymodule.fetch', AsyncMock(return_value=42)):
        result = await mymodule.fetch()
        assert result == 42
standing in for an async function records
Using a Single Event Loop Across Multiple Tests (Loop Reuse)
Sharing one event loop across the entire test session causes subtle state leakage. Tasks started in one test can still be pending when the next test begins. Cleanup hooks registered with
or unawaited tasks can fire at unexpected times and pollute later tests.
. Each test should get a fresh event loop:

# pytest.ini
[pytest]
asyncio_mode = auto

# conftest.py — correct: each test gets a new loop
import pytest
import asyncio

@pytest.fixture
def event_loop():
    loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
    yield loop
    loop.close()
, giving every test its own loop. Only opt into a wider scope (
) if you have a compelling reason and understand the state-sharing consequences.
Not Cancelling Background Tasks After Tests

inside application code persist until they are cancelled or complete. If a test creates tasks (directly or by calling application code that does), and those tasks are not cleaned up, they print warnings and may affect subsequent tests:

Task was destroyed but it is pending!
Task exception was never retrieved
Always cancel and await background tasks in teardown:
must be caught and suppressed here; re-raising it or letting it propagate will fail the fixture teardown.
by default cancels all remaining tasks when one raises an exception, then re-raises the first exception. Tests that do not account for this behavior can produce confusing failures:

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_gather():
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        failing_task(),
        succeeding_task(),
    )
    # If failing_task raises, succeeding_task is cancelled
    # results is never assigned — the exception propagates instead
When testing scenarios where some tasks should fail and others should not, use

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_gather_with_errors():
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        failing_task(),
        succeeding_task(),
        return_exceptions=True,
    )
    assert isinstance(results[0], ValueError)
    assert results[1] == "ok"
gives back either all results or raises — never a mixed list.
Race Conditions That Only Appear in Tests
Async code is deterministic in a single-threaded event loop — tasks run to their next

point before another task gets control. This means the order tasks execute is determined by when they are created and scheduled, which can differ between production (where many concurrent events arrive) and tests (where you control exactly what runs when).
A common pattern is testing code that assumes a specific task ordering that only holds in the test environment:

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_order_dependent():
    task_a = asyncio.create_task(write_value())
    task_b = asyncio.create_task(read_value())
    await asyncio.gather(task_a, task_b)
    # BUG: assumes task_a always runs before task_b
    # In tests with a controlled event loop this may always hold
    # In production with real I/O it may not
The fix is to make the ordering explicit using
, queues, or by structuring the test to avoid relying on implicit scheduling order. An
to run to its next suspension point before
is created, but this is fragile and couples the test to event-loop internals. Use explicit synchronisation instead:

value_written = asyncio.Event()

async def write_value():
    # ... do work ...
    value_written.set()

async def read_value():
    await value_written.wait()
    # ... now safe to read ...

Without explicit timeouts, a coroutine that never returns (waiting on a network call, a lock that is never released, or a queue that never receives an item) will hang the entire test suite indefinitely.
does not impose a default timeout.
on expiry and cancels the wrapped coroutine. Catching
in tests lets you assert that slow code is properly bounded:

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_operation_respects_timeout():
    with pytest.raises(asyncio.TimeoutError):
        await asyncio.wait_for(slow_operation(), timeout=0.1)
Patching at the Wrong Import Level

, the target must be the name as it is looked up in the module under test, not the name as it is defined. This is the standard mock patching rule, but it causes more confusion in async code because the error manifests as a
about awaiting, not as a plain "wrong value" assertion failure.

# mymodule.py
from external_lib import fetch   # imports fetch into mymodule's namespace

# test_mymodule.py — WRONG: patches external_lib.fetch but mymodule already has its own reference
with patch('external_lib.fetch', AsyncMock(return_value=42)):
    await mymodule.do_work()

# CORRECT: patches the name mymodule uses
with patch('mymodule.fetch', AsyncMock(return_value=42)):
    await mymodule.do_work()
Debugging Async Code: Tools and Techniques
This activates extra runtime checks: slow callbacks (default threshold 100ms), unawaited coroutines, and tasks that take too long to cancel. Set it before running tests:

import asyncio
asyncio.get_event_loop().set_debug(True)

# Or via environment variable
# PYTHONASYNCIODEBUG=1 pytest

async def debug_tasks():
    tasks = asyncio.all_tasks()
    for t in tasks:
        print(t.get_name(), t.get_coro())
makes log output from concurrent tests readable. Assign meaningful names when creating tasks:
backends and has cleaner task scope semantics, which eliminates some of the loop-reuse and task-leak problems described above.
) when mocking coroutine functions. A sync mock causes
— the test will silently pass without executing. Use
Give every test its own event loop
— and always cancel background tasks in fixture teardown to avoid cross-test state leakage.
to surface unawaited coroutines that would otherwise pass silently.
, synchronous file I/O, CPU-heavy loops) with async equivalents inside async tests.
on any operation that could block indefinitely, and use
Patch at the import level used by the module under test
, not at the definition site — this is the single most common cause of mocks that appear to work but have no effect.
